assignment_teachersNote:
★ Plutarch/Government: Grade 9 (U.S.) — TERM 1 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Tell me everything you remember about the life of Julius Caesar.

· You are a Roman citizen who lived through all of it. Caesar was on the wrong side as a young man, nearly drowned escaping, nearly starved at Pharsalus, made himself dictator for life, died in the Senate chamber, and was declared a god three days later. The city burned. The men who killed him were hunted to their deaths. Now it is quiet. Was Rome better or worse for having had him? Why?

· You are someone who admired Caesar deeply and also someone who could see clearly what he was becoming. Those two things were both true at the same time for most of the people closest to him. Think about a person in your own life, or a figure in history, or a character from a story, in whom you can see greatness and destructive behavior running alongside each other. What do you do with admiration that you cannot separate from concern?

· You have followed Caesar from his early campaigns through every battle, every crisis, every triumph, and into Rome at the end. You have watched him grab sailors, swim through fire, feed horses seaweed, pardon enemies, refuse bodyguards, and finally cover his face and stop when he saw Brutus. Looking at the whole of what you witnessed: what did this man actually want, and did he ever get it?

★ Plutarch/Government: Grade 9 (U.S.) — TERM 2 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Tell me everything you remember about the lives of Agis and Cleomenes.

· You have watched Agesilaus, Agis's own uncle, embrace the reform publicly, help burn the debt bonds in the marketplace, laugh at the "brightest fire" he had ever seen, and then delay the land division once, and again, and again, until the moment was lost and Leonidas came back and Agis was killed in prison. Agesilaus was not a tyrant. He was not an enemy of Sparta. He was a debtor who did not want to pay. What do you think about his behavior?

· You are a Spartan woman. You watched Agesistrata and Archidamia killed in the same prison where Agis died. You watched Cratesiclea sail to Egypt as a hostage, forcing back her tears at the temple door so her son would not see her break. You watched Agiatis, widow of a murdered king, keep her husband's dream alive and pass it on to the man she was forced to marry. You watched Chilonis choose grief twice instead of safety once. The city is quiet now. Antigonus came and went without burning anything down. Was what these women carried worth what it cost them?

★ Plutarch/Government: Grade 9 (U.S.) — TERM 3 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Tell me everything you remember about the lives of the Gracchi.

· The brothers were nothing alike, and everything alike. Tiberius was gentle and restrained. Gaius was fiesty. Think of a time when you had to choose between patience and urgency, when both paths seemed right, but for different reasons. Which did you choose? What happened? Which brother do you think you are more like, and which one do you think you should try to be?

· You have followed the Gracchi from Cornelia's house through every assembly, every law, every riot, and into the silence after. You watched Tiberius weep before his father's statue on the last morning of his life and keep walking. You watched Gaius pull his hand quietly from his wife's grip at the door and not look back. You watched Cornelia lose both sons and go on hosting dinner parties for learned men without shedding a tear. What do you think about their behavior? Is there a right way to face grief, loss and fear?
